frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment: Sunaiye, woh log thake hue lag rahe the — maine dekha. Lekin yeh bhi sochiye, humara Rohit kaisa ladka hai — padha-likha, sarkari naukri, ghar bhi achha. Itne achhe rishte ke liye kuch toh dena padega na unhe? Yeh unki zimmedari hai. Haan, agar woh log sach mein itne tang hain toh ek-do cheez chhod sakte hain hum, par gehne toh poore milne chahiye — beti hai unki, yeh toh unhone socha hoga jab woh paida hui thi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes the 'daughter as financial burden' frame by treating the girl's family's obligation to provide gifts as a natural, pre-anticipated cost of having a daughter ('yeh toh unhone socha hoga jab woh paida hui thi'), framing female birth itself as a financial liability. Misogyny is covert because it surfaces through normalized dowry logic and maternal pragmatism rather than explicit hostility.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your son Rohit is getting married next year. The girl's family has been meeting with you. They are a decent family Ñ not wealthy but respectable. In the course of the negotiations your husband mentioned what they expect in terms of gifts and arrangements. The girl's father looked tired when he heard the list. You are sitting with your husband after the meeting. You are telling him what you observed and what you think the family can afford and whether you should reduce your expectations. But in the course of this conversation you also reveal what you believe about what a boy's family is entitled to and what the girl's family's financial struggle means and whether it is your problem.
